annotations.label: First incompleteness theorem (lead statement)

anchor.snippet: no consistent system of axioms whose theorems can be listed by an effective procedure

annotations.note: Mathlib's FirstOrder logic has no syntactic proof system or provability predicate, so Gödel's first incompleteness theorem is not stated.

annotations.label: Second incompleteness theorem (lead statement)

anchor.snippet: no such system can demonstrate its own consistency

annotations.note: There is no formalized consistency/provability predicate or second incompleteness theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Church's theorem (Entscheidungsproblem unsolvable)

anchor.snippet: Church 's proof that Hilbert's Entscheidungsproblem is unsolvable

annotations.note: The unsolvability of the Entscheidungsproblem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Formal system

anchor.snippet: a formal system is a deductive apparatus that consists of a particular set of axioms

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Theory` (a set of axiom sentences) and `Language`, but no deductive apparatus (inference rules / derivations).

annotations.label: First-order Peano arithmetic as a formal system

anchor.snippet: One example of such a system is first-order Peano arithmetic

annotations.note: Mathlib defines no first-order theory of Peano arithmetic (only the Presburger language and ACF/DLO theories).

annotations.label: Cannot have all three properties

anchor.snippet: systems which contain a sufficient amount of arithmetic cannot possess all three of these properties

annotations.note: This is the incompleteness theorem itself, which is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Effectively axiomatized system

anchor.snippet: A formal system is said to be effectively axiomatized

annotations.note: Mathlib has recursive-enumerability (`REPred`) in computability but never links it to first-order theories as effective axiomatization.

annotations.label: Effectively generated theories

anchor.snippet: Examples of effectively generated theories include Peano arithmetic and Zermelo–Fraenkel set theory

annotations.note: No effectively-generated PA or ZFC theory is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: True arithmetic

anchor.snippet: The theory known as true arithmetic consists of all true statements about the standard integers

annotations.note: Mathlib's `completeTheory M` (Th(M)) generalizes true arithmetic, but it is not instantiated to the standard model of arithmetic.

annotations.label: Syntactic completeness

anchor.snippet: A set of axioms is ( syntactically , or negation -) complete if

annotations.note: `Theory.IsComplete` (satisfiable and deciding every sentence or its negation) is the model-theoretic form of negation-completeness.

annotations.label: Gödel's completeness theorem

anchor.snippet: Gödel proved that first-order logic is semantically complete

annotations.note: Mathlib has compactness but no syntactic provability relation, so the soundness/completeness equivalence (⊢ ↔ ⊨) is not stated.

annotations.label: Euclidean geometry without parallel postulate is incomplete

anchor.snippet: Euclidean geometry without the parallel postulate is incomplete

annotations.note: Mathlib has no first-order axiomatization of Euclidean geometry to discuss its completeness.

annotations.label: Dense linear orders incomplete

anchor.snippet: the theory of dense linear orders is not complete

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `dlo` and proves it ℵ₀-categorical (hence complete) without endpoints, related to but not asserting the article's incompleteness of bare DLO.

annotations.label: Continuum hypothesis unprovable in ZFC

anchor.snippet: The continuum hypothesis is a statement in the language of ZFC that is not provable within ZFC

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Cardinal.continuum` but neither the continuum hypothesis as a statement nor its independence from ZFC.

annotations.label: Peano arithmetic is not complete

anchor.snippet: Peano Arithmetic is not complete

annotations.note: Incompleteness of PA is not formalized (and PA is not even defined as a theory in Mathlib).

annotations.label: No consistent extension of PA is complete

anchor.snippet: no effectively axiomatized, consistent extension of Peano arithmetic can be complete

annotations.note: This restatement of incompleteness is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Consistency

anchor.snippet: A set of axioms is (simply) consistent if there is no statement

annotations.note: Mathlib's `IsSatisfiable` (theory has a nonempty model) is the semantic analog of consistency; syntactic consistency is not defined.

annotations.label: PA consistent from ZFC but not itself

anchor.snippet: Peano arithmetic is provably consistent from ZFC, but not from within itself

annotations.note: Neither PA's consistency nor metatheoretic consistency proofs are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Inaccessible cardinal yields a model of ZFC

anchor.snippet: ZFC + "there exists an inaccessible cardinal " proves ZFC is consistent

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Cardinal.IsInaccessible` but no V_κ-is-a-model-of-ZFC formalization or any Con(ZFC) statement.

annotations.label: Consistent iff has a model

anchor.snippet: a theory is consistent if and only if it has a model

annotations.note: Mathlib *defines* consistency as having a model (`IsSatisfiable`), so the substantive completeness-theorem biconditional with syntactic consistency is absent.

annotations.label: All statements as axioms is inconsistent

anchor.snippet: If one takes all statements in the language of Peano arithmetic as axioms

annotations.note: This illustrative example over the (absent) PA language is not formalized.

annotations.label: Unrestricted comprehension inconsistency

anchor.snippet: Additional examples of inconsistent theories arise from the paradoxes

annotations.note: Russell's paradox / unrestricted comprehension is not formalized as an inconsistent theory in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Robinson arithmetic suffices

anchor.snippet: One sufficient collection is the set of theorems of Robinson arithmetic

annotations.note: Robinson arithmetic (Q) is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Algebraically closed fields complete

anchor.snippet: The theory of algebraically closed fields of a given characteristic is complete

annotations.note: `ACF_isComplete` proves the theory of algebraically closed fields of characteristic p is complete.

annotations.label: Euclidean geometry complete, consistent, effective

anchor.snippet: Euclidean geometry itself (in Tarski's formulation) is an example of a complete, consistent, effectively axiomatized theory

annotations.note: Tarski's axiomatization of Euclidean geometry and its completeness are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Presburger arithmetic

anchor.snippet: The system of Presburger arithmetic consists of a set of axioms for the natural numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the Presburger *language* (0,1,+) but the theory and its completeness/decidability are explicitly left as TODO.

annotations.label: Willard's self-verifying systems

anchor.snippet: Dan Willard ( 2001 ) has studied some weak families of arithmetic systems

annotations.note: Willard's self-verifying systems are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Principle of explosion

anchor.snippet: an inconsistent set of axioms will prove every statement in its language

annotations.note: `models_sentence_iff` makes an unsatisfiable theory (no models) vacuously entail every sentence, the semantic counterpart of explosion.

annotations.label: No complete consistent finite axiom list

anchor.snippet: a complete and consistent finite list of axioms can never be created

annotations.note: This corollary of incompleteness is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First Incompleteness Theorem (Rosser form)

anchor.snippet: Any consistent formal system F within which a certain amount of elementary arithmetic can be carried out is incomplete

annotations.note: The first incompleteness theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel sentence

anchor.snippet: is often referred to as "the Gödel sentence" for the system F

annotations.note: No Gödel sentence construction exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extending F by its Gödel sentence stays incomplete

anchor.snippet: It is possible to define a larger system

annotations.note: This iterated-incompleteness argument is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gödel sentence has simple syntactic form

anchor.section: Syntactic form of the Gödel sentence
anchor.snippet: the Gödel sentence can be written in the language of arithmetic with a simple syntactic form

annotations.note: No Gödel sentence and hence no analysis of its Π₁ syntactic form is formalized.

annotations.label: MRDP rewriting of the Gödel sentence

anchor.section: Syntactic form of the Gödel sentence
anchor.snippet: the Gödel sentence can be re-written as a statement that a particular polynomial in many variables with integer coefficients never takes the value zero

annotations.note: The Diophantine rewriting of the Gödel sentence is not formalized (and the full MRDP theorem is absent).

annotations.label: Gödel sentence is true

anchor.snippet: the Gödel sentence is, in fact, true

annotations.note: Truth of the Gödel sentence in the standard model is not formalized.

annotations.label: Gödel sentence false in nonstandard models

anchor.snippet: the Gödel sentence will be false in some nonstandard models of arithmetic

annotations.note: No nonstandard-model behavior of the Gödel sentence is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: The liar paradox

anchor.snippet: The liar paradox is the sentence

annotations.note: The liar paradox is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Tarski's undefinability theorem

anchor.snippet: cannot be represented as a formula of arithmetic. This result, known as Tarski's undefinability theorem

annotations.note: Tarski's undefinability of truth is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: ω-consistency

anchor.snippet: A system is ω-consistent if it is not ω-inconsistent

annotations.note: ω-consistency is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: ω-consistency implies consistency

anchor.snippet: The ω-consistency of a system implies its consistency, but consistency does not imply ω-consistency

annotations.note: Neither ω-consistency nor its relation to consistency is formalized.

annotations.label: Rosser's strengthening

anchor.snippet: strengthened the incompleteness theorem by finding a variation of the proof

annotations.note: Rosser's trick / strengthened incompleteness is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Consistency formula Cons(F)

anchor.snippet: it is possible to canonically define a formula Cons( F ) expressing the consistency of F

annotations.note: No arithmetized consistency formula Cons(F) exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Second incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: the consistency of F cannot be proved in F itself

annotations.note: The second incompleteness theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: PA proves largest consistent subset consistent

anchor.snippet: first-order Peano arithmetic (PA) can prove that

annotations.note: This subtlety about provability of consistency variants is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hilbert–Bernays provability conditions

anchor.snippet: the provability predicate Prov A ( P ) satisfies the Hilbert–Bernays provability conditions

annotations.note: No provability predicate or Hilbert–Bernays conditions are formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: PA verifies the Hilbert–Bernays conditions

anchor.snippet: Peano arithmetic, however, is strong enough to verify these conditions

annotations.note: Verification of the provability conditions in PA is not formalized.

annotations.label: F1 cannot prove consistency of F2 proving F1

anchor.snippet: cannot prove the consistency of any system

annotations.note: This consequence of the second incompleteness theorem is not formalized.

annotations.label: PRA cannot prove consistency of PA

anchor.snippet: Thus PRA cannot prove the consistency of PA

annotations.note: PRA and unprovability of Con(PA) within it are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gentzen's consistency proof

anchor.snippet: Gerhard Gentzen proved the consistency of Peano arithmetic in a different system

annotations.note: Gentzen's ε₀-induction consistency proof of PA is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wellfoundedness of ε₀

anchor.snippet: the ordinal called ε 0 is wellfounded

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Ordinal.epsilon` (with `ε₀ = epsilon 0`); wellfoundedness is automatic for ordinals as a class, but its specific role as a transfinite induction principle proving Con(PA) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Undecidable (proof-theoretic sense)

anchor.snippet: that of a statement being neither provable nor refutable in a specified deductive system

annotations.note: With no proof system, Mathlib has no notion of a sentence being independent (neither provable nor refutable).

annotations.label: CH and AC undecidable

anchor.snippet: The continuum hypothesis can neither be proved nor refuted in ZFC

annotations.note: Independence of CH and AC from ZFC is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Whitehead problem undecidable (Shelah)

anchor.snippet: the Whitehead problem in group theory is undecidable

annotations.note: Shelah's independence result for the Whitehead problem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chaitin's incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: there is an upper bound c such that no specific number can be proved in that system to have Kolmogorov complexity greater than

annotations.note: Kolmogorov complexity and Chaitin's incompleteness theorem are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Paris–Harrington principle

anchor.section: Undecidable statements provable in larger systems
anchor.snippet: the Paris–Harrington principle , a version of the infinite Ramsey theorem , is undecidable

annotations.note: The Paris–Harrington principle and its independence from PA are not formalized.

annotations.label: Goodstein's theorem

anchor.section: Undecidable statements provable in larger systems
anchor.snippet: Goodstein's theorem , a statement about sequences of natural numbers

annotations.note: Goodstein's theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kruskal's tree theorem

anchor.section: Undecidable statements provable in larger systems
anchor.snippet: Kruskal's tree theorem , which has applications in computer science, is also undecidable from Peano arithmetic

annotations.note: Kruskal's tree theorem is not in Mathlib (the existing `KruskalKatona` file is the unrelated Kruskal–Katona theorem).

annotations.label: Graph minor theorem (Robertson–Seymour)

anchor.section: Undecidable statements provable in larger systems
anchor.snippet: The related but more general graph minor theorem

annotations.note: The Robertson–Seymour graph minor theorem (well-quasi-ordering of finite graphs under the minor relation) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Halting problem undecidable

anchor.snippet: the halting problem is undecidable

annotations.note: `ComputablePred.halting_problem` proves that deciding whether a code halts on input n is not computable.

annotations.label: Matiyasevich's theorem

anchor.snippet: Matiyasevich proved that there is no algorithm

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes a key MRDP step (`Dioph.pow_dioph`, the power function is Diophantine) but not the full theorem or the undecidability of Hilbert's tenth problem.

annotations.label: Effectively generate an unprovable polynomial

anchor.snippet: it is possible to effectively generate a multivariate polynomial

annotations.note: This incompleteness-via-Diophantine-equations construction is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recursively inseparable sets proof

anchor.snippet: the existence of recursively inseparable sets can be used to prove the first incompleteness theorem

annotations.note: Recursively inseparable sets are not defined in Mathlib's computability library.

annotations.label: Cantor's diagonal argument

anchor.section: Proof sketch for the first theorem
anchor.snippet: Cantor's diagonal argument

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes Cantor's diagonal argument as `Function.cantor_injective` (no injection from the powerset back into the set).

annotations.label: Gödel number

anchor.snippet: every formula or statement that can be formulated in the system gets a unique number, called its Gödel number

annotations.note: Mathlib injectively encodes terms and formulas (and via `Encodable` to ℕ), a general analog of Gödel numbering, though not Gödel's specific scheme.

annotations.label: ASCII encoding example

anchor.snippet: The word hello is encoded as 104-101-108-108-111 in ASCII

annotations.note: This illustrative ASCII example is not a formal statement and is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Statement form (class-sign)

anchor.snippet: is called a statement form or class-sign

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Formula` (a formula with free variables) generalizes the notion of a statement form/class-sign, but not in Gödel's arithmetic-specific sense.

annotations.label: Provability formula Bew(y)

anchor.snippet: there is a statement form Bew ( y ) that uses this arithmetical relation

annotations.note: No arithmetized provability formula Bew(y) exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Provable p implies Bew(G(p)) provable

anchor.snippet: if a statement p is provable in the system then

annotations.note: This provability-representability property is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Diagonal lemma

anchor.snippet: the diagonal lemma , which says that for any sufficiently strong formal system and any statement form F there is a statement p

annotations.note: The diagonal (fixed-point) lemma of arithmetic is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: p is undecidable

anchor.snippet: the statement p is undecidable in our axiomatic system

annotations.note: The undecidability of the Gödel sentence is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Boolos's Berry-paradox construction

anchor.snippet: for any computably enumerable set S of true sentences of arithmetic, another sentence which is true but not contained in

annotations.note: Boolos's Berry-paradox proof of incompleteness is not formalized in Mathlib.
